data.title: M&T Bank: Improved Credit Quality Highlights A Strong Q3
data.description:
Shares of M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 50% to a 52-week-high. That rally appears set to continue Thursday, with the stock gaining a further 4% in response to solid quarterly earnings. I last covered MTB in July, rating shares a “hold,” though given their 10% gain vs. the market’s 5% rally, a “buy” would have been justified. I remain a hold.

In the company’s third quarter, M&T earned $4.08, beating estimates by $0.46. This was a strong quarter with expense discipline and impressive net interest performance. Credit trends are particularly heartening, increasing my confidence in the sufficiency of its reserves. Non-interest expenses were up a modest 2%, helping to sustain operating margins. Deposits were the one blemish on the quarter, in my view, but even here, the data is mixed rather than negative. In other words, underlying results support the positive reaction we are seeing in shares today.

Looking first at net interest trends, interest income gained $8 million to $1.74 billion in Q3. This increase was aided by 1 extra calendar day. Additionally, net interest margin (NIM) expanded by 3bps to 3.62%. Last quarter, there was a 7bp gain due to unexpected payments on non-accrual loans, so some moderation was to be expected. Increasing further despite the lapse of this tailwind was a positive.

On the liability side, there was a 2bps benefit from lower funding costs, as it continues to pay down higher-cost wholesale funding. Additionally, interest-bearing deposit costs fell 2bps. During the quarter, the average Fed funds rate fell by ~5bps, implying a 40% flow-through to deposits, (levels above 35% are solid). It is encouraging to see M&T bring down deposit costs alongside short-term interest rates, though the absolute level of deposits remains underwhelming.

As you can see below, M&T had $161.5 billion of average deposits, down $2 billion sequentially. It did grow checking accounts while letting more higher-cost CDs mature, a positive mix shift. Non-interest-bearing (NIB) deposits fell a further $1.5 billion, an acceleration from last quarter’s decline. This is an area where I am hoping to see banks near their floor, and some, like Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) are showing decelerating outflows. It appears M&T has not yet found the floor to these balances.

Interestingly, while average balances declined by $2 billion, on an end of period basis, deposits grew by $4 billion to $164.6 billion. This was because deposit momentum was weak at the start of the quarter but finished more strongly. It will be important to see this momentum continue. M&T has a stable deposit base, but over time, deposit growth is critical to growing assets and earnings power.

Given lower deposits, there was a 1.2% decline in interest-bearing assets to $191.4 billion. MTB was able to grow NII even with fewer assets because of higher yields on its asset base. It registered 3bps of benefit from fixed reinvestment. M&T has $31 billion of securities, up $1.3 billion. With improved liquidity and capital, it has been able to grow this portfolio again. Thanks to higher short-term yields than on maturing securities, its portfolio yield was up 9bps to 3.7%. It has a 3.6-year average duration, limiting mark-to-market exposure from swings in interest rates.

Average loans were essentially flat at $134.8 billion, but there was a meaningful mix shift here. CRE loans declined by 8% or $2.4 billion, while C&I loans increased by 3% or $1.6 billion; consumer loans also grew. M&T has significantly pulled back underwriting new commercial real estate loans, allowing these balances to decline as it focuses more on business lending.

This effort is delivering results beyond just loan composition; it is helping improve my confidence around loan quality. M&T has $10.9 billion of criticized C&I and CRE loans, down $1.2 billion sequentially. I would note that 96% remain current, but these are loans that have a higher risk of defaulting. Declining balances suggest potential losses in a downturn will be smaller than previously feared by regulators.

This decline is consistent with other metrics of credit quality. M&T took 35bps of charge-offs, down 6bps from Q2. As a consequence, it took $120 million in provisions for credit losses, down $30 million sequentially and from last year. With this level of provisioning, it held reserves flat. As you can see below, non-accrual loans fell for a second straight quarter and are now lower than a year ago. This decline stands in contrast to banks with more consumer and credit card exposure, where quality has continued to soften.

Because non-accruals are falling, but it held reserves flat, coverage is improving. With $2.2 billion of allowances, that gives M&T 114% coverage, which is still a bit light. If delinquencies were to rise again, I would expect MTB to resume building reserves. However, if we see an economic soft landing that keeps delinquencies at similar levels, M&T should be able to maintain a $110-$140 million quarterly provision rate.

Importantly, even if losses tick up a bit, M&T is well capitalized. It has a common equity tier 1 capital ratio (CET1) of 11.5%, up 0.1% sequentially. This enabled $200 million of buybacks, a pace that may increase next year. Its 2.9% dividend yield is also secure.

Forward guidance was reasonable, in my view. It expects a similar level of NII and a similar NIM. I would note that its average deposit range of just $160+ billion, implies flat to lower average balances, as in Q3. Given the EOP build, this is a bit disappointing. At this time, I am hesitant to assume sustained deposit growth until we see it. Still, MTB is improving the profitability of its existing asset base, given fixed reinvestment and loan repositioning. It expects to do another $200 million of buybacks.

Back in July, I felt M&T had $15-$15.50 in forward earnings power. Having progressed 3 months further and seen more encouraging data on credit quality. I now see forward earnings of about $15.50-$16.25. This assumes a flat total asset base and flat deposits at current levels. That gives shares a forward multiple of 12x.

We have seen multiple expansion across the sector over the past few months, given the market rally and lessened recession fears. With strong credit quality, this premium multiple to peers with more CRE exposure is justified. However, I believe to grow earnings more quickly and widen its multiple further, we will need to see deposit growth accelerate, which remains an outstanding question. As such, I remain a “hold” on M&T Bank Corporation stock. It has greatly improved the efficiency of its asset base, lifting shares, but with that theme largely played out, growing its asset base will be critical to extending the rally.

data.title: My 9.5% Income Portfolio-Fifty Shades Of Blue
data.description:
Pontormo and Mannerism

Behind the Ponte Vecchio in Florence, at the beginning of the road leading to the Pitti Palace, there is a small square overlooked by the church of Santa Felicita. We are in the Oltrarno district, the area of the city located on the left bank of the Arno River, enlivened by old stores and small cafes hidden in picturesque alleys in which a genuine Florentine atmosphere can still be breathed.

The church is an integral part of the so-called Vasari Corridor, a sheltered walkway that connects Palazzo Vecchio with Palazzo Pitti via the Uffizi and over the Ponte Vecchio. It is the heart of Medici Florence, inside which is preserved the Deposition by Jacopo Carucci, known as Pontormo (1494-1557), a painter of the Florentine school who pioneered Mannerism.

Mannerism was the artistic current, of refined and nonconformist style, that marked the end of the Renaissance, and Pontormo’s tempera on panel painting (datable to around 1526-28) represents a founding masterpiece of it. His composition lacks any spatial reference: there is no landscape but an aerial space with a profusion of light and evanescent figures that seem to fly, occupying nearly every portion of the surface.

Beyond the composition, however, the charm of this painting lies in its soft, pale, suffused hues. The chiaroscuro is almost absent, and consequently the perception of volumes is reduced, diluted in the reference of colors, among which pinks, blues, greens, and faded yellows prevail. All soft, delicate tones, but so many shades in those light blues!

Let’s Take a Break

Every time I stop to look at this work, as well as others by Pontormo, I wonder how many tones his mind was capable of perceiving to offer such luminous and extraordinarily balanced compositions. All this, while outside the church of Santa Felicita flows the frenetic, bipolar world of today. A world that does not allow nuances between different ideas, opinions and tastes, on the big issues of life as on small condominium disputes.

Even in the field of investments, schools of thought confront and oppose each other, sometimes without being able to find shareable syntheses. As for me, I consider this space that Seeking Alpha grants me as an agorà, the main square of ancient Greek cities, in which to gather to discuss the issues that unite us, first and foremost in how to succeed in making the most of our savings with the instruments made available by the market. In my case CEFs, ETFs, a few BDCs and an ETN. I am not so experienced as to venture out to research individual stocks, so I am “content” with what I can get out of these securities, which in any case is not little.

Over the years, I have gradually developed my own method of selection, based primarily on NAV performance, but to catch my breath I have decided to set aside my convictions on this subject for once and focus today on the market pricing of my beloved funds. Basically, I decided for this article to focus on price, comparing it with the lows and highs touched by each security in my portfolio over its history. I will use this as a way to provide an overview of the path each one has taken from launch to current market quotation and a personal assessment of the possible room for maneuver with respect to those values, while not losing sight of its relationship to NAV. Price is what you pay (according to the mood of the moment,) but NAV always represents what you really buy!

My Overall Portfolio

As you may know, my assets today include 28 securities (19 CEFs, 5 ETFs, 3 BDCs, 1 ETN) organized into three different portfolios:

Cupolone Income Portfolio (named after Brunelleschi’s Florentine dome) consists of sixteen CEFs with monthly distributions:

  • BlackRock Science And Technology Trust (BST)
  • Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income (CCD)
  • Calamos Global Total Return (CGO)
  • Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II (EOS)
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Adv. Global Dividend Opps (ETO)
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Adv. Dividend Income (EVT)
  • Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities (GOF)
  • John Hancock Tax-Adv. Dividend Income (HTD)
  • Pimco Corporate & Income Strategy (PCN)
  • Pimco Dynamic Income (PDI)
  • John Hancock Premium Dividend (PDT)
  • Pimco Corporate & Income Opportunities (PTY)
  • Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty (RQI)
  • Special Opportunities Fund (SPE)
  • Cohen & Steers Infrastructure (UTF)
  • Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG)
  • XAI Octagon FR & Alt Income Term Trust (XFLT)

Giotto Income Portfolio (named after the fourteenth-century Florentine painter and architect) includes four ETFs and one ETN with monthly distributions that adopt a covered-call strategy:

  • JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (JEPI)
  • JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income (JEPQ)
  • Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call (QYLD)
  • Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call (RYLD)
  • Credit Suisse X Links Crude Oil Shares Covered Call ETN (USOI)
  • Global X S&P 500 Covered Call (XYLD)

Masaccio Income Portfolio (named after the founder of Renaissance painting) contains three BDCs and two CEFs with quarterly distributions:

  • Ares Capital Corp (ARCC)
  • Crescent Capital (CCAP)
  • Fidus Investment Corp (FDUS)
  • Barings Corporate Investors (MCI)
  • Royce Value Trust (RVT)

Cupolone Income Portfolio

BST today has a purchase price located roughly midway between the 2016 low and the 2021 highs, after a period low ($28.07) touched during 2022. The long-term trend appears to be upward, with ample room for ascent toward the all-time highs, and the fact that it trades at a discount of -3.37 to NAV makes it in my eyes quite an interesting fund.

CCD has a purchase price located about 25 percent below the highs touched in 2021, after a descent that lasted almost two years and the sharp rebound in recent months. The margins for recovery seem clear even if the very strong premium on NAV (+30.48%) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying it at the moment.

CGO has a purchase price located 38 percent below the highs touched in the years following its launch. The fund has shown some vitality over time, with alternating highs and lows characterized by notable recoveries, albeit with a progressively downward trend. The slight premium to NAV (+1%) does not make me imagine much convenience in buying it, even though in recent months it has moved decisively away from the period lows ($7,90) touched a few months ago. A fund on which, however, I am progressively lightening my position, with the intention of closing it out.

EOS today has a purchase price located about 12% below the highs marked in 2021, after a steep descent and a pretty convincing ascent, propelling it back toward those highs. The trend appears to be upward, and its slight discount to NAV (-4.22%) makes it a rather attractive fund in my eyes.

ETO today has a purchase price located 27.5 percent below its all-time highs in the years since its launch. In 2021, the stock marked a period high at $33.80, toward which it is climbing back up after a descent that lasted a couple of years. The trend appears to be upward, and its excellent discount to NAV (-8.88 percent) makes it very attractive in my eyes.

EVT today has a purchase price located roughly 20 percent below its all-time highs in the years since its launch. In 2021, the stock marked a period high at $30.08, toward which it is climbing back up after a descent that lasted a couple of years. The trend appears to be upward, and its excellent discount to NAV (-9.27 percent) makes it a very attractive fund in my eyes.

GOF has a purchase price located about 35% below its all-time highs, touched more than a decade ago, after a more than two-year descent from the period high marked in 2021 ($21.98) and the recent rebound. The very strong premium to NAV (+31.94 percent) does not make me imagine a convenience in buying, although with the rate cut it may have further room for recovery. However, all the previous highs appear to me very distant and difficult to repeat.

HTD today has a purchase price located roughly 17 percent below the all-time highs marked on the eve of the pandemic. After rising between 2021 and 2022, the stock marked a period low in the fall of 2023, from which it began a convincing climb toward all-time highs. The trend appears to be upward, and its discount to NAV (-6.35%) makes it a very attractive fund in my eyes.

PCN has a purchase price located about 25 percent below the high touched on the eve of the pandemic, which was then followed by a second, slightly lower high in the summer of 2021. Since then a two-year descent began, interrupted in recent months by a rebound that brought it to its current values. The strong premium to NAV (+6.20 percent) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying, although with the rate cut it may have further room to recover.

PDI has a purchase price located about 27% above the lows touched in recent months, after a descent that lasted more than two years and a recent rebound. However, the pre-pandemic highs appear very distant and difficult to repeat, as well as the last period high, marked in 2021 ($29.60). The strong premium on the NAV (+14.34%) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying this fund, although with the rate cut it could have further margins of recovery.

PDT today has a purchase price located approximately 30 percent below the all-time high, marked on the eve of the pandemic. After the descent in 2020 and a strong recovery during 2021, the stock marked a period low in the fall of 2023, from which it started a discrete rebound. The trend appears to be upward, and its slight discount to NAV (-3.98%) makes it fairly attractive in my eyes.

PTY has a purchase price located about 35 percent below the high touched a decade ago. After the recent period high ($21.66) in the summer of 2021, the stock then slipped to a new low ($11.50) in the fall of 2022, from which it started a rebound that brought it to its current values. The strong premium to NAV (+23.72%) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying this fund, although with the rate cut it may have further room to recover.

RQI today has a purchase price located roughly 47 percent below its all-time high since its launch. After a disastrous crash at the time of the Great Recession, the stock embarked on a long and steady recovery that led it to a period high at $18.22 in January 2022. The subsequent downturn, which lasted a couple of years, brought it to a low of $9.17 from which a slow recovery began. The trend appears to be upward, and its slight discount to NAV (-1.94 percent) makes it a fairly attractive fund in my eyes.

SPE today has a purchase price located roughly 25 percent below its all-time high, set a decade ago. The outbreak of the pandemic brought it to all-time lows, from which it initiated a very strong rebound followed by a subsequent descent to new period lows ($10.26) about a year ago. Since then a recovery has begun that has brought it to its current levels. The trend appears to be upward, and its very strong discount to NAV (-13.57%) makes it a rather attractive fund in my eyes.

UTF today has a purchase price located approximately 14 percent below its all-time highs, marked in the summer of 2021, after which it started a descent that brought it to its period lows ($18.36) about a year ago. Since then a recovery has begun that has brought it to its current levels. The trend appears to be upward, and despite quoting more or less at par with NAV, it is a very interesting fund in my eyes, given also the proximity to its absolute highs.

UTG today has a purchase price located about 18 percent below the all-time highs marked on the eve of the pandemic. After a new period high ($35.44) in the summer of 2021, the stock then started a descent to a low of $23.52, from which it rebounded vigorously to its current level. The trend appears to be upward, and despite quoting at a slight discount to NAV (-1,41%), it is a very attractive fund in my eyes, apparently projected toward its absolute highs.

XFLT has a purchase price more than 40% below the highs touched at launch in 2017, though far from the 2020 lows. The albeit modest premium on NAV (+2.56 percent) does not make me imagine a convenience in buying it, given also the current sideways phase in a sagging picture since launch and in my eyes with very little hope of reversal. For this reason I decided to progressively lighten my position, with the intention of closing it out.

Giotto Income Portfolio

JEPI today has a purchase price located about 6 percent below the highs marked in January 2022. After a decline that brought it to a period low of $50.85 in the fall of 2022, the fund has started a sharp recovery to current levels. The trend appears to be upward toward absolute highs, making it very attractive in my eyes.

JEPQ today has a purchase price located less about 2 percent below the highs marked just three months ago. After a decline that brought it to a period low of $49.66 in early August, the fund has started a recovery to its current levels, propelling it toward absolute highs and making it extremely attractive in my eyes.

QYLD has a purchase price almost 30 percent below the highs touched at launch in fall 2013, although it has moved away from the lows ($15.36) of fall 2022. The current recovery attempt bodes well, although the successive relative highs over time (which, moreover, have always been declining) still appear far away and difficult to repeat, in a sagging picture since launch.

RYLD has a purchase price more than 36 percent below the highs touched after launch and nearly replicated ($25.67) in the fall of 2022. Since then it has begun a slow and inexorable descent to current values, with an all-time low of $15.18 in early August 2024. The overall picture looks very compromised and, in my eyes, leaves very little hope for a turnaround. For this reason I decided to progressively lighten my position, with the intention of closing it out.

USOI has lost almost 90 percent from its maximum in 2019. There is not much to say about it, except that its presence in my portfolio is tied solely and exclusively to the fact that it is the only security whose “coupons” (as the management house calls them) allow me to offset past losses: let'say I pretend they are “tax free” for me. However, juicy coupons such as the one paid in September ($2.1248) justify in my eyes the maintenance of a modest position in my portfolio.

XYLD has a purchase price about 21 percent below the two all-time highs touched in 2018 and then on the eve of the pandemic. After a new period high at $51.07 in January 2022, the fund has since fallen twice around period lows below $38. The current recovery attempt bodes well, although the three successive highs over time (which, moreover, have been declining) still appear distant and difficult to repeat.

Masaccio Income Portfolio

ARCC has a purchase price about 4.5 percent below the highs touched in January 2022. After a slight decline, the trend appears to be recovering toward absolute highs, making it a very attractive stock in my eyes.

CCAP has a purchase price 11 percent below the highs touched in fall 2021. After a fair downturn, the trend appears to be recovering toward absolute highs, making it a very attractive stock in my eyes.

FDUS has a purchase price about 12 percent below the highs touched a decade ago. After the collapse occurred at the outbreak of the pandemic, the stock bolted a convincing recovery that brought it within sight of those highs. Then, in January 2022, it initiated a sideways phase that brought it to its current values. The trend appears to be upward and makes it very attractive in my eyes. For this reason I plan to increase my position with the money from the funds I decided to liquidate, hoping to purchase it at at a lower price.

MCI today has a purchase price located about 37 percent below the all-time high, which was moreover marked with a spike almost fifteen years ago. Otherwise, the fund’s path has been steadily upward, up to the current level that represents the highest point since the aforementioned all-time high. Despite the high premium to NAV (+17.18 percent) the fund looks very attractive in my eyes, although this is probably not the best time to buy it. I plan to increase my position with the money from the funds I decided to liquidate, hoping that the premium can be reduced.

RVT today has a purchase price located about 33 percent below the all-time high, marked on the eve of the Great Recession, which took its price to absolute lows. Since then, a long and convincing recovery to current values has begun, after the significant drop at the outbreak of the pandemic and the prodigious recovery the following year, which propelled it well above ($19.92) today's price. The trend appears to be upward, and its decent discount to NAV (-10.33%) makes it a fairly attractive fund in my eyes.

The Final Brushstroke

There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio, than are dreamt of in your philosophy.” (Hamlet, 1.5.167-8)

Just as we cannot agree on the best movie, the best actor or the greatest jazz musician, even less do we share the criteria on the basis of which we make our investment choices. Each of us has a personal method of selecting securities for inclusion in our portfolios, based on a wide variety of parameters. There are dozens of them, all of which are certainly valid and proven, but as far as I am concerned, I am unable to appreciate the importance and validity of each of them. Let’s say that by necessity I have had to restrict my range of choices, thus reducing my color palette.

Over time, in fact, I decided to simplify my approach by adopting a method of selection based primarily on NAV performance, although today I have chosen to devote my attention only to historical price trends of my securities. A choice that, in this case, I would call “monochromatic,” consciously far from the variety of shades and the extraordinary coloristic effects of Pontormo and other great Mannerist painters, such as Rosso Fiorentino or the Sienese Domenico Beccafumi.

Personal note: These days the olive mills in my area are opening because the olive harvest to make new oil begins. Autumn, which the English Romantic poet John Keats called “season of mists and mellow fruitfulness,” is one of the most beautiful times of the year. Should you ever happen to visit Florence at the end of October, I suggest you to take an excursion into the hills or to the Chianti region and enter an olive mill. It is a wonderful experience amid colors, scents and flavors that tell a thousand-year old history.

data.title: Is EMB Really The Best ETF To Invest In Emerging Market Bonds? VWOB Might Be Better
data.description:
When considering the emerging bond market and seeking a passive fund solution to invest in it, the first choice is undoubtedly the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMB). With an AUM of $15.53B, EMB is the largest passive fund designed to invest in the emerging bond market. The fund was established in 2009, and in my opinion, over the past 15 years, no truly competitive fund has emerged when it comes to sovereign and quasi-sovereign emerging bonds.

Therefore, I would like to start by analyzing the most well-known passive fund in the emerging bond market (EMB), and then examine its competitors in the sovereign or quasi-sovereign segment to see if there is indeed no better option than EMB.

My studies will lead to an interesting conclusion: the largest funds may not always be the best funds. In 2013, another passive fund was introduced, which initially seemed too risky, but its performance over the past 10 years suggests a different scenario: I'm speaking about Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VWOB).

What is the peer of EMB?

Let's start by analyzing EMB. First, I’d like to begin with a comparison analysis, asking myself: what is the true peer in the market?

These are the peers proposed by Seeking Alpha, but I would like to modify that list:

I'm adding the Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond (VWOB) and SPDR® Bloomberg Emerging Markets Local Bond ETF (EBND) while excluding the iShares J.P. Morgan EM Corporate Bond ETF (CEMB) and WisdomTree Emerging Markets Corporate Bond Fund ETF (EMCB). This is because I want to focus on funds primarily investing in sovereign bonds, which are issued by the governments of emerging market countries.

If you're interested in a comparison of corporate emerging market bonds, let me know in the comments section.

Now I'm going to look at the performance chart:

It’s easy to see that the best performer is the Global X Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBD), mainly because its holdings include a higher corporate component, whereas EMB invests in sovereign and quasi-sovereign entities. This is reflected in their differing credit quality exposure:

iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMB)

Global X Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBD)

So, I'm excluding EMBD from the comparison analysis, also because its AUM is significantly lower than other solutions in the market, not to mention that the fund is relatively new compared to its competitors.

For the same reason, I will also exclude the First Trust TCW Emerging Markets Debt ETF (EFIX) and the SPDR® DoubleLine® Emerging Markets Fixed Income ETF (EMTL) from my selection.

This is the final performance chart comparison, and we can see that, over the long term, the composition of EMB and VWOB outperformed EBND.

Let's do a quick comparison between the total returns:

So, despite EBND having the highest dividend yield (gains from interest, which I will refer to as dividend yield from now on) at around 5.63%, it had the lowest total return over the long term. Why? This is probably due to the inconsistency in its 'dividend yield' growth history.

At the same time, EBND invests in local currency-denominated bonds from emerging markets. This means that investors are exposed to fluctuations in emerging market currencies relative to the U.S. dollar.

For this reason, I’m excluding it from my selection.

EMB VS VWOB

And what about VWOB? The performance between EMB and VWOB is not significantly different. VWOB offers a higher dividend yield than its peers, around 5.67%, while EMB’s yield is 4.79%. Additionally, VWOB has a lower expense ratio than EMB: approximately 0.20% compared to 0.39% for EMB

The credit quality distribution is also quite different.

VWOB Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond Index Fund ETF Shares

It's important to remember that VWOB tracks the Bloomberg USD Emerging Markets Government RIC Capped Index, which focuses solely on sovereign debt from emerging market governments, unlike EMB. This point deserves particular attention, in my opinion. It means that VWOB includes in its portfolio sovereign bonds with lower ratings and may also invest in smaller, riskier emerging market countries, maintaining higher geographic diversification but, in reality, presenting more risk. This explains why the dividend yield is higher. Indeed, EMB has a more selective exposure to major emerging markets, potentially avoiding smaller and more volatile countries. Its focus on quasi-sovereign bonds provides additional stability.

Why in my opinion it's better choosing VWOB than EMB

In my portfolio, I chose to add VWOB instead of EMB for several reasons:

  • When considering a long-term investment, I’m well aware of the impact that the expense ratio has on final performance year over year, and VWOB's 0.20% expense ratio is the lowest in the market.
  • Even though EMB’s AUM is higher than VWOB’s, we’re talking about the two largest passive funds in the emerging market space, and there is absolutely no liquidity issue with either fund.
  • VWOB’s dividend yield is higher than EMB’s, and since its launch, VWOB has demonstrated good consistency in this regard

  • Even though VWOB holds bonds considered riskier than those in EMB’s portfolio (sovereign and quasi-sovereign bonds), the difference in standard deviation between the two funds is minimal (EMB standard deviation: 8.33; VWOB standard deviation: 8.39). However, over the last 10 years, the total return difference between these two funds is around 4%.

  • Lastly, a personal opinion: if I choose a passive fund that is truly focused on government emerging markets, the choice should go directly to VWOB. This is because the holdings of that ETF represent the entire emerging market debt, while EMB concentrates its investments in the sovereign and quasi-sovereign segments.

Risk

What’s the risk? Remember that VWOB has exposure to lower-rated sovereign bonds and may include smaller, riskier emerging market countries, while EMB invests in sovereign and quasi-sovereign bonds, meaning bonds issued by government-related entities in addition to national governments. Theoretically, during geopolitical issues, EMB should offer more stability, as its exposure should result in lower volatility. However, history has shown that this isn’t necessarily true. We’ve already discussed this, and the standard deviation doesn't demonstrate a significantly better risk coverage by EMB. For example, during periods of global market stress (such as the 2013 taper tantrum or the 2020 COVID-19 crisis), the drawdown of these two funds was quite similar.

Conclusion

Starting with the largest emerging market government bond ETF, EMB, we conclude with VWOB, the passive fund that, in my opinion, is the best on the market. VWOB offers a good combination of characteristics, including a lower expense ratio, higher dividend yield, better performance returns, and a consistent composition of holdings in the portfolio. In my opinion, it is a strong choice when looking to invest in this financial sector.
